title: Agents of Dreamland by Caitlin R. Kiernan - Dungeon Dive Book Club Review
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Gs03wWJ6vFc

The Dungeon Dive Book Club's first review. Daniel (@dungeondive) rereads Caitlín R. Kiernan's Agents of Dreamland and loves it even more the second time — enough to build a personal timeline of its fractured chronology, which runs from Edgar Rice Burroughs dreaming of a fungal cloud beyond Pluto in 1933 to centuries in the future, all inside 124 pages. The review is really an essay on demanding fiction. Kiernan compresses that vast scope by strategically omitting connective tissue and trusting the reader to arrive equipped — with the Cthulhu mythos, with Yuggoth, and above all with the 1990s: Art Bell, remote viewer Courtney Brown's claim that a companion object trailed comet Hale-Bopp, and the Heaven's Gate cult who believed it was their ride home. Daniel, who listened to those broadcasts live, unpacks how the novella's Children of the Next Level are patterned directly on Ti and Do's followers. His one caveat follows from the same strength: without that esoteric background, the book might read as baffling rather than brilliant. His favourite chapter — the Signalman as a boy watching monster movies in August 1968 — gets singled out as a small masterpiece of nostalgia. The live Google Meet discussion is scheduled, and two candidate September picks are teased.

----

Kiernan expects the reader to bring the Mythos and the '90s with them. Do you enjoy books that demand homework, or should a story carry all its own weight?
